Active-Pleadings â€” the complaint had been filed and summons issued, but no answer or motion had been filed yet
Active-Pleadings â€” the motion to dismiss had been filed (tolling the answer deadline) but had not yet been ruled on

Yes â€” the motion to dismiss was filed February 10, tolling the answer deadline. It remained tolled until the motion was denied on March 18.
Yes â€” discovery opened March 18 when the motion to dismiss was denied, per LR 7.2

7 days â€” the discovery deadline was August 15, the stay began August 8 (corrected date), leaving 7 days. The initial incorrect stay date of August 5 would have left 10 days.
Active-Pleadings â€” discovery had opened on March 18, but the answer had not yet been filed (filed March 31). Status did not change to Active-Discovery until the answer was filed.

No â€” LR 7.2 states that only the judge may grant a discovery extension; stipulations between the parties are insufficient without a court order. The May 1 stipulation extended only the interrogatory response deadline, not the overall discovery deadline.
February 13, 2026 â€” 30 calendar days from the corrected service date of January 14. (The uncorrected date of January 12 would have produced February 11.)
April 1, 2026 â€” 14 calendar days from the March 18 denial order
July 16, 2026 â€” 120 calendar days from March 18 (discovery opening date)
30 days granted (not the 45 days requested). New deadline: August 15, 2026.
September 15, 2026 â€” the stay began August 8 with 7 days remaining. The stay lifted September 8. 7 days from September 8 = September 15.
October 15, 2026 â€” 30 calendar days from the September 15 discovery close
Yes â€” filed June 20, which is 26 calendar days before the July 16 deadline. More than 5 business days.
January 19, 2027 to March 5, 2027 â€” between 45 and 90 calendar days from the December 5 dispositive motion denial
Yes â€” February 23, 2027 is 80 calendar days from December 5, within the 45-to-90-day window
October 31, 2026 â€” 21 calendar days from the October 10 filing. Sycamore filed October 28, within the deadline.
November 11, 2026 â€” 14 calendar days from the October 28 response. Orion filed November 8, within the deadline.
September 18, 2026 â€” 10 remaining days from September 8 instead of 7. This is why the correction matters.

Service date corrected from January 12 to January 14. This changes the original answer deadline from February 11 to February 13. The motion to dismiss (filed February 10) was filed before either deadline, so the correction does not change the tolling analysis.
The Clerk initially docketed a 45-day extension (the requested amount). The judge's order grants 30 days only. The discovery deadline is August 15, not August 30.

The stay was initially docketed as effective August 5 (the date of Vega's settlement notice). The stay order is dated August 8. Corrected to August 8. This changes remaining discovery days from 10 to 7 and the post-stay deadline from September 18 to September 15, which cascades through the dispositive motion deadline and the trial-setting window.

The trial date itself (February 23, 2027) is set by the judge and falls within the window under either calculation. Under the corrected stay date, the discovery close is September 15, dispositive deadline is October 15, denial is December 5, and the 45-90 day window is January 19 to March 5. Under the uncorrected date, discovery would close September 18, potentially shifting all downstream dates by 3 days, but the judge set the trial based on the corrected timeline.

The Clerk noted (in Dutch) that the calculation of the resumed discovery deadline depends on the correction. With August 5, 10 days remain; with August 8, 7 days remain. The difference is significant and affects all subsequent deadlines.
